content: A particle of mass $$10 \mathrm{~kg}$$ moving eastwards with a speed $$10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ collides with another particle of the same mass moving northward with the same speed $$10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$. The two particles coalesce on collision. The new particle of mass $$20 \mathrm{~kg}$$ will move in the north-east direction with velocity.
explanation:
Here, initial momentum of particle $$=$$ Final momentum of particles,

$$m v \hat{\mathbf{i}}+m v \hat{\mathbf{j}}=2 m v$$

Where, $$\mathbf{v}=\frac{v}{2}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})$$

Hence, $$v=\frac{v}{2} \times \sqrt{2}=v / \sqrt{2} \quad\left(\because v=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right)$$

So, $$v=\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$

content: A projectile is launched vertically from the earth with speed $$v_1$$ hits a satellite at the height $$h$$ moving with speed $$v_2$$. If both have the same mass $$m$$, then what is the common velocity if they move together after the collision?
explanation:
Since, momentum remains conserved for a system so,

$$p$$ (before collision) $$=p$$ (after collision)

$$m \mathbf{v}_1+m \mathbf{v}_2=2 m \mathbf{v}_c$$

Where, $$\mathbf{v}_c=$$ common velocity after collision In $$x$$-direction,

$$m v_2=2 m v_{c, x}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad v_{c, x}=\frac{v_2}{2}$$

Similarly, in $$y$$-direction, $$v_{c, y}=\frac{v_1}{2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Resultant common velocity,

$$\left|\mathbf{v}_c\right|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{v_1}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{v_2}{2}\right)^2}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}$$
